coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-assisted degradation of some common biogenic amines: An in vitro study
coredata.dc:creator: Ghorbani, Mahdi
Molaei, Rahim
Moradi, Mehran
Tajik, Hossein
Salimi, Fatemeh
Kousheh, Seyedeh Alaleh
Koutamehr, Mahmoud Esmaeili
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the reduction/degradation capability of carbon dots (CDs) on biogenic amines (BAs) produced by Enterococcus faecalis and Escherichia coli was investigated in vitro conditions. In this work, the response surface methodology (RSM) and the central composite design (CCD) was applied in order to find the optimized CDs concentration and incubation time on the reduction of BAs. CDs represented a quite high degradation percentage on cadaverine, followed by histamine, tyramine and putrescine. Cadaverine produced by E. coli was completely degraded, while CDs degraded only 55% of putrescine produced by E. faecalis. Based on RSM outputs, both independent variables (concentration of CDs and the incubation time), affected the reduction/degradation of BAs in the two investigated pathogens. The results revealed that the concentration of CDs had the major influence on BAs degradation. The antimicrobial and FTIR investigations implied that CDs degraded only BAs of E. faecalis and have no activity on the production of BAs by the bacterium, In the case of BAs of E. coli, CDs not only inhibited the E. coli growth and reduced BA accumulation but also degraded the as-formed BAs. These data serve to reinforce a conclusion that CDs represent a potential material for BAs degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biodegradation
Foodborne pathogens
Antibacterial activity
Carbon quantum dots
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation of media and bacterial cultures
The mechanisms of BA reduction by CDs
Mechanisms of BA reduction by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Contour graph and reciprocal effects of CDs concentrations (A) and incubation time (B) of E. coli (left) and E. faecalis (right) on the tyramine degradation.
Contour graph and reciprocal effects of CDs concentrations (A) and incubation time (B) of E. coli (left) and E. faecalis (right) on the putrescine degradation.
Contour graph and reciprocal effects of CDs concentrations (A) and incubation time (B) of E. coli on the histamine degradation.
Contour graph and reciprocal effects of CDs concentrations (A) and incubation time (B) of E. coli (left) and E. faecalis (right) on the cadaverine degradation.
Antibacterial activity (reported as the zone of inhibition; mm) of CDs solution against E. coli and E. faecalis according to agar well-diffusion.
FTIR spectra of CDs and BAs samples before and after exposing with CDs.
The levels of two independent variables used for CCD modeling a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: A fixed incubation temperature of 37 ± 1 °C was applied during the experiment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The descriptive modeling statistics used to check the usefulness of the model applied to every BAs.
Numerical optimization and total net errors obtained from predicated and observed responses (%BAs degradation).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-assisted degradation of some common biogenic amines: An in vitro study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, 1177, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, 1177, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the reduction/degradation capability of carbon dots (CDs) on biogenic amines (BAs) produced by Enterococcus faecalis and Escherichia coli was investigated in vitro conditions. In this work, the response surface methodology (RSM) and the central composite design (CCD) was applied in order to find the optimized CDs concentration and incubation time on the reduction of BAs. CDs represented a quite high degradation percentage on cadaverine, followed by histamine, tyramine and putrescine. Cadaverine produced by E. coli was completely degraded, while CDs degraded only 55% of putrescine produced by E. faecalis. Based on RSM outputs, both independent variables (concentration of CDs and the incubation time), affected the reduction/degradation of BAs in the two investigated pathogens. The results revealed that the concentration of CDs had the major influence on BAs degradation. The antimicrobial and FTIR investigations implied that CDs degraded only BAs of E. faecalis and have no activity on the production of BAs by the bacterium, In the case of BAs of E. coli, CDs not only inhibited the E. coli growth and reduced BA accumulation but also degraded the as-formed BAs. These data serve to reinforce a conclusion that CDs represent a potential material for BAs degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Biogenic amines (BAs) are a group of active biological compounds that are known as nitrogen bases with low molecular weight. Based on their structure, BAs are classified into aliphatic (e.g., putrescine and cadaverine), aromatic (e.g., tyramine and phenyl ethylamine), and heterocyclic (e.g., histamine) amines (Papageorgiou et al., 2018). In addition, another classification has been proposed based on the number of amine groups including monoamines (e.g., phenylethylamine, tyramine, etc.), diamines (e.g., histamine, putrescine, and cadaverine), and polyamines (spermidine and spermine) (Spano et al., 2010). They are mainly formed through the decarboxylation of amino acids and to a lesser extent, by the amination/transamination of aldehydes-ketones. The principal requirements for the formation of BAs in food are the presence of free amino acids and appropriate conditions for the microbial growth and production of decarboxylase (Molaei, Tajik, Moradi, & Forough, 2019). The main microbial groups associated with BAs production are some Gram-negatives (Enterobacter spp. and Pseudomonas spp.) and Gram-positive bacteria, for example, Staphylococcus spp., Bacillus spp. and LAB (Molaei, Tajik, & Moradi, 2019). Some species of Lactobacillus spp., Pediococcus spp., Lactococcus spp. and Streptococcus spp. usually have decarboxylase enzymes and have been cited as responsible for BA accumulation in the fermented foods (Barbieri, Montanari, Gardini, & Tabanelli, 2019). Over the recent years, many approaches have been proposed to minimize the accumulation of BAs in food products including high hydrostatic pressure, irradiation, modified atmosphere packing, ozone treatment, amine oxidizing agents, starter cultures, and plant extracts (Kuley et al., 2019; Kuley & Özogul, 2011; Özogul, Kaçar, & Kuley, 2015).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel group of nanomaterial comprised of hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, and nitrogen with less than 10 nm in size. They have attracted a lot of attention owing to being eco-friendliness, magnificent fluorescence properties, desirable water solubility, proper permeability in cells, low toxicity, and easy and cost-effective synthesis (Namdari, Negahdari, & Eatemadi, 2017). Several studies have been carried out in order to investigate the antibacterial activity of CDs on various bacterial strains such as Escherichia coli (Shahshahanipour, Rezaei, Ensafi, & Etemadifar, 2019), Listeria monocytogenes (Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020), Klebsiella pneumoniea, Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Jhonsi et al., 2018), and Bacillus subtilis (Li et al., 2018). The high surface/volume ratio and small size of CDs help them to inhibit the bacterial growth. They also produce free radical and oxidizing agents (Shahshahanipour et al., 2019) and disrupt or change the membrane permeability of the cell wall (Li et al., 2018). Substantial degradation features of CDs have been reported on antibiotics (Kumar et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019), methylene blue (Jusuf, Sambudi, Isnaeni, & Samsuri, 2018), and organic dyes (Cheng et al., 2019).

The current BAs reduction strategies are based on the inhibition of microorganisms responsible for the formation of BAs and suppression of microbial decarboxylases (Lee, Kim, Her, Kim, & Lee, 2018). There have been numerous studies concerning BAs reduction/degradation by lactic acid bacteria (LAB) (Alvarez & Moreno-Arribas, 2014; Herrero-Fresno et al., 2012; Tittarelli, Perpetuini, Di Gianvito, & Tofalo, 2019) and LAB postbiotics (Moradi et al., 2020) with potential technological interest for food application. However, there is a lack of studies of nanomaterial performance on the reduction and degradation of BAs. As a new approach, the direct action of CDs on BAs has been investigated for the first time in the current study. The objectives of this work were to 1) examine the influence of CDs on the reduction of BAs (histamine, tyramine, putrescine, and cadaverine) of two well-known BA-producing bacteria (Enterococcus faecalis and E. coli) using the response surface methodology (RSM) modeling with the purpose of modelization, and 2) investigate the possible mechanisms of action of CDs on the reduction of four BAs in vitro conditions.

Black mulberry (Morus nigra) was employed for the green fabrication of CDs by one-step hydrothermal carbonization according to the method described in our previous work (Salimi, Tajik, Molaei, & Moradi, 2019). Highly dispersed CDs with the size of less than 10 nm were synthesized and exploited in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of media and bacterial cultures
ce:section.ce:para: An amino-acid broth containing 3 g/L yeast extract, 5 g/L peptone, 1 g/L MgSO4, 0.5 g/L MnSO4, 1 g/L glucose (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany), and 3 g/L of correspondent amino acids (histidine, tyrosine, and lysine; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) was prepared and applied as the main BA-producing medium in this study (Pircher, Bauer, & Paulsen, 2007). All ingredients, except glucose, were dispensed in an Erlenmeyer flask containing distilled water and sterilized by autoclaving for 15 min at 121 °C. The pH of the broth was adjusted to 6.7 by 1 mol/L NaOH/HCl, and glucose was separately sterilized and added into the main broth. The frozen (−85 °C in 25% glycerol) stock cultures of E. coli (ATCC 25922), and E. faecalis (ATCC 29212) were obtained from the microbial culture collection of the Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Urmia University. Both bacteria were cultured into tryptic soy (TS) broth (Quelab Laboratories; Montreal, Quebec, Canada) at 37 ± 1 °C for 18 h. The final cell concentration was adjusted to ~108 CFU/mL by spectrophotometer (OD600 nm = ~0.1) and the viable count was estimated by plating on TS agar.

The RSM model is used for optimization of considered variables for better outcomes in an experiment (Moradi, Mardani, & Tajik, 2019). In this work, the RSM and the central composite design (CCD) was applied in order to find the optimized CDs concentration and incubation time on the reduction of BAs via Design-Expert Software Version 11.1.0.1 (Stat ease Inc, Minneapolis, USA). For this purpose, bacterial suspension (~107 CFU/mL; 0.5 mL) from each bacterial strain was added into the amino-acid broths (4.5 mL) containing five different concentrations of CDs and then incubated at 37 ± 1 °C (Özogul et al., 2015) for five different contact times (Table 1 ). The experiments comprised 28 randomized runs which CDs concentrations and contact times were the independent factors and CDs degradation (%) as the response. The minimum and maximum levels of independent variables were selected according to the preliminary experiments.

The sample preparation and derivation of BAs was done according to a previously explained procedure (Kuley, Balikci, Özogul, & Cengiz, 2013) with some minor modification. At designed contact times, each sample was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min and the supernatant was decanted into conical centrifuge tubes. Then, the trichloroacetic acid solution (2 mL; 6% V/V; Sigma-Aldrich) was added into each tube, centrifuged (4000 rpm for 10 min), and finally filtered through a 0.45 μm PTFE filter. The derivation process was performed as follows: 4 mL of the filtrated bacterial supernatant was mixed in 1 mL of NaOH (2 mol/L) and the mixture was vortexed for 30 s. Then, the mixture was refrigerated (4 ± 1 °C) for 20 min followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min. Thereafter, 40 μL of benzoyl chloride were added, vortexed for 30 s, and allowed to complete the derivation reaction at room temperature for 10 min. Sodium chloride (2 mL saturated solution) was applied to stop the benzoylation reaction. In the next step, the derivated analytes were extracted two times with diethyl ether. The organic layer (4 mL) was removed and dried by a constant stream of N2 gas. Later, the residues were dissolved in 1 mL of acetonitrile and stored at 4 ± 1 °C until analysis.

HPLC analysis was performed by an Agilent 1200 series system (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA) equipped with an online vacuum degasser unit (G1322A), a quaternary pump (G1311A), an auto-sampler (G1329A), and a UV detector (G1314B). The analytical column used was XDB-C18 (15 × 0.46 cm id., 5 μm) connected with a guard column of (4.6 × 12.5 cm id., 5 μm). ChemStation (Chem32) software was applied for instrument control, data acquisition, and data processing. The HPLC analysis was done isocratically using a combination of ultrapure water and acetonitrile with a ratio of 60:40 (v/v) pumped at a flow rate of 1 mL/min at ambient temperature. The injection volume was 5 μL and the detection was monitored at 198 nm. Between each run, the column was re-conditioned with the mobile phase for 10 min. The identification and quantification of BAs in the medium were performed using retention time and peak area, respectively. The BA-degradation percentage of CDs was determined using the following formula (Eq. (1)).
(1)
% Degradation =
C Control
−
C CDs
C Control
× 100
where the CCDs and Ccontrol are the samples containing the corresponding BAs and CDs and samples without CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of BA reduction by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate how CDs reduce the BAs contents in vitro conditions, two approaches were examined. Firstly, the antimicrobial effectiveness of CDs on individual BAs forming microorganisms were carried out by the agar well diffusion method. The adjusted inocula (~106 CFU/mL) were swabbed on the surface of a plate containing the Mueller Hinton agar. Subsequently, wells having a diameter of 8 mm were punched an agar and filled with 100 μL of CDs. The agars were then incubated under aerobic conditions and the diameters of inhibition zones were measured after a 24-h incubation at 37 ± 1 °C.

In the second approach, the direct action of CDs on commercially available (Sigma-Aldrich) BAs by the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was investigated. For this purpose, the designed samples containing target BAs (10 ppm), and optimized CDs concentration were prepared and after incubation at optimized time, the extraction process was conducted and their absorption spectra were recorded using a Thermo Nicolet instrument (Nexus® 670, USA) during wavenumber range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 1 cm−1. The data were processed with Omnic software (version 6.0; Thermo Electron, Madison, WI, USA).

Some foodborne pathogens such as Bacillus spp., E. coli, Clostridium, Salmonella, Aeromonas hydrophila, L. monocytogenes, E. faecalis, and K. pneumonia have the ability to produce BAs and ammonia (Kuley et al., 2019; Toy, Özogul, & Özogul, 2015). The capability of E. coli (ATCC 25922) and E. faecalis (ATCC 29212) to produce BAs has been previously investigated (Özogul et al., 2015). The content of histamine, tyramine, putrescine, and cadaverine produced by E. faecalis (non-detected, 13.59, 2.89 and 1.95 mg/L, respectively) and E. coli (1.01, 3.52, 11.79 and 5.16 mg/L, respectively) after a 72-h incubation process in the current work were somewhat different to that found in the mentioned report. Similar to the current work, no histamine-producing activity has been previously reported for E. faecalis ATCC 29212 (Özogul et al., 2015).

Recent studies mainly focus on the usage of LAB (Zhang, Lin, & Nie, 2013) and natural agents (Lee et al., 2018) to reduce the levels of BAs in the real food (Zhang et al., 2013) in vitro conditions (Kuley & Özogul, 2011; Özogul et al., 2015; Özogul, Simat, Gokdogan, Regenstein, & Özogul, 2018). In this work, we examined the influence of CDs on the degradation of histamine, tyramine, putrescine, and cadaverine using the RSM modeling (Table S1). The CCD design and the reduced cubic model were employed for all BAs (Table 2 ), depicting a cubic relationship between BAs reduction and CDs concentration/bacterial incubation time. The non-significant lack of fit and the high R2 (ranged from 0.895 to 0.989) values in all experiments on BAs confirms the validity of the model. Moreover, the fact that the difference between the adjusted and predicated R2 was less than 0.2 showed the reliably of the data for interpolation (Table 2). The F-value in the model compares the mean square of the source to the residual mean square and the obtained values suggest that the models are significant.

The role of LAB on the degradation of BAs was also reported (Tittarelli et al., 2019). In a study, two LAB strains (L. casei and E. casseliflavus) isolated from a ewe's cheese represented high degradation activity (~60%) on histamine, without having any impact on decarboxylase activity (Tittarelli et al., 2019). Contour graphs and the reciprocal effects of CDs concentrations and bacterial incubation time on different BAs degradation have been presented in Figs. 1–4
. Generally, CDs represented the highest degradation (80–100%) percentage on cadaverine followed by histamine (80–90%), tyramine (70–90%), and putrescine (55–80%). Interestingly, cadaverine produced by E. coli was completely degraded, while CDs degraded only 55% of putrescine produced by E. faecalis. A concentration- and incubation-dependent degradation activity was also demonstrated. Both independent variables, especially CDs concentration, affect the degradation of BAs in the two investigated pathogens either separately or in combination. The final proposed equations for fitted models are as follow:

E. Faecalis: Tyramine degradation (%) = −25.67 + 13.72X1 − 0.75X2 − 0.06X1X2
Putrescine degradation (%) = −56.13 + 19.20X1 − 2.38X2 + 0.03 X1X2
Histamine degradation (%) = +39.32–0.02X1 − 0.01X2 + 0.06 X1X2
Cadaverine degradation (%) = −5760.50 + 753.29X1 + 43.29 X2 + 47.25 X1X2 − 56.69X1 2 − 3.32X2 2

E. coli:Tyramine degradation (%) = −35.96 + 17.67X1 − 1.02 X2 − 0.06 X1X2
Putrescine degradation (%) = +45.62 + 3.43X1 − 0.64X2 − 0.02 X1X2
Histamine degradation (%) = + 81.34–0.02X1 − 0.04X2 + 0.01 X1X2
Cadaverine degradation (%) = −14211.48 + 1958.63X1 + 1183.35X2 − 9.10 X1X2 − 67.59X1 2 − 8.08 X2 2
where coded values, represents concentration (X1) and incubation time (X2).

Based on the modeling outputs, one optimization solution (combination) with the desirability of 1 (CDs at 12% concentration and 48 h incubation time) was selected and then predicted and observed responses at optimum solution provided by RSM are shown in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of BA reduction by CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, CDs have attracted more interest due to their multiple functional features (Shauloff, Bhattacharya, & Jelinek, 2019). Antimicrobial activity is a promising characteristic of CDs for food applications. Different CDs employ diverse antimicrobial mechanisms of action. They may target the cell membrane integrity, disturb the secondary structures of bacterial genomic elements, and hinder gene expressions in the cell (Li et al., 2018), the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by transferring O2 molecule, and production of free radicals (Jhonsi et al., 2018).

During the normal metabolism in a mammalian cell, degradation of BAs occurs by aminooxidases that deaminate the corresponding BA and produce aldehyde, ammonia, as well as hydrogen peroxide and finally excrete them in the form of urine (Wunderlichová, Buňková, Koutný, Jančová, & Buňka, 2014). However, in vitro conditions, the reduction activity of the agent in the content of BAs can be related to 1) the direct inhibitory activity on the growth of BA-producing bacteria and 2) the degradation of as-prepared BAs (Tittarelli et al., 2019; Xie, Wang, Deng, & Xu, 2016). To investigate the possible mechanism, in the first part, the antimicrobial activity of CDs was investigated using the well diffusion method against both pathogens to find out whether CDs can directly inhibit BAs-producing bacterial strains. The obtained results (Fig. 5 ) showed that CDs have no antimicrobial activity against the BA-producing Gram-positive bacteria (E. faecalis ATCC 29212), while the lower zone of inhibition (11.12 ± 0.12 mm) was reported for the Gram-negative ones (E. coli ATCC 25922). This implies that as-prepared CDs have no direct effect on the production of BAs by the bacterium; consequently, it only degraded as-formed BAs of E. faecalis. On the other hand, CDs represent a weak dual-action on the as-formed BAs of E. coli, in the form of the reduction of BAs accumulation by the bacterium and degradation. In an interesting study, the inhibitory and stimulatory activity of the cell-free supernatant (CFS) of some LAB on the production of tyrosine by different food-borne pathogens in tyrosine decarboxylase broth were investigated (Toy et al., 2015). In a similar work, Xie et al. (2016) analyzed the performance of the CFS of L. plantarum and came to the conclusion that the CFS mainly inhibits the cell growth of BA-positive bacteria and reduces the BA accumulation. Furthermore, it was verified that the CFS cannot inhibit the activity of decarboxylases (Xie et al., 2016).

Several factors can contribute to the interaction between CDs and bacterial cells including the surface functional groups of CDs and the charge and bacterial specifications (the structure and charge of the cell wall). The degree of the electrostatic repulsion between CDs and the bacterial cell can be measured by measuring the zeta potential. The zeta potential indicates the net electrical charge and is dependent on the charges of CDs and the bacterial cell surface. The electric charge of bacterial cells differs based on the kind of bacterial species and the culture medium being investigated. The zeta potential of E. coli ATCC 25922, and E. faecalis ATCC 29212 are −4.95 mV and −16 to −32 mV, respectively. Owing to the presence of negative groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl), the zeta potential of fabricated CDs was −44.5 mV. Consequently, both bacteria, especially E. faecalis, were repulsed by CDs. Moreover, due to the structural differences, gram-negative bacteria are more resistant to the H2O2, which is the most important ROS (Russell, 2003). In this sense, some bacterial strains, including E. coli, have a mechanism to remove significant concentrations of ROS (i.e., H2O2) and grow in such an environment (Oladeinde et al., 2018).

In order to evaluate the second hypothesis that BAs reduction is connected to the direct interaction of CDs and BAs without acting on bacterium or decarboxylase, the FTIR spectra of CDs, BAs, and a mixed sample of each BAs were studied after being exposed with the CDs under optimized conditions. The spectral absorption band of CDs (Fig. 6 D; the black spectrum) confirms that the surface of the CDs contains a variety of functional groups including O–H and N–H, C–H (aliphatic and aromatic), CO, and C–O–C bonds which their characteristic bands appeared at, 3446, 3067, 2927, 1724, 1266, and 1096 cm−1, respectively. On the other hand, the FTIR spectrum of the sample containing histamine (Fig. 6-A; red spectrum) clearly depicted the absorption peaks located at 3421, 3067 and, 2929 cm−1, which could be assigned as ν(N–H) and ν(C–H) bonds moieties of histamine. In addition, the strong and sharp bands which appeared at 1786, 1725, and 1215 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations (scissoring mode) of N–H and the stretching vibration of the C–N (primary and secondary amines) in the structure of aliphatic, aromatic, and heterocyclic BAs (Yadav & Mukherjee, 2017).

On the other hand, the spectrum shown in magenta color belongs to the cadaverine sample (Fig. 6-B). The main peaks at 3303 cm−1 (N–H stretching vibration), 2929 and 2859 cm−1 (asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations), 1541 and 1450 cm−1 (C–H bending vibrations and CH2 twisting vibration, respectively), and 1266 cm−1 (C–N stretching vibrations) can affirm the vibrational frequency of cadaverine (Ortega, Macuil, Gayoú, & Morán, 2014).

In the sample containing putrescine (Fig. 6-C; green spectrum), the spectrum analysis shows that the spectral band located at 3454, 1727, and 1637 cm−1 are attributed to stretching and bending vibration of the N–H bond (Ortega et al., 2014), and the bands at 2928 and 2865 cm−1 could be ascribed to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of C–H bonds in the putrescine structure. Moreover, the other characteristic peaks at 1530, 1451, and 1268 cm−1 correspond to the C–H bending vibrations, the CH2 bending (twisting) vibration, and the C–N stretching vibrations in the target analytes.

In addition, in the spectrum of the sample containing tyramine (Fig. 6-D; blue spectrum), the stretching and bending vibrations of the amino group occur in wavenumbers 3434 cm−1, 1786 cm−1 (scissoring mode), and 1726 cm−1 (twisting modes) (Yadav & Mukherjee, 2017). Other main characteristic absorptions related to the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds appeared in wavenumbers 2928 and 2861 cm−1 and the bending vibrations were found in the wavenumber region 1600-1450 cm−1 (Siddiqui et al., 2009). The C–O and C–N stretching vibration in the tyramine structure could be observed in the vicinity of 1250 cm−1 (1266 and 1215 cm−1).

Based on the obtained data, and through comparing the spectra obtained after the application of the CDs, it seemed that the spectral peaks of the BAs have been changed and the recorded spectra for the treated samples were very similar to the CDs spectrum. Accordingly, two possible hypotheses were proposed 1) BAs were degraded by the direct action of CDs on BAs and the production of different unknown fractions, and/or 2) CDs attach to the structure of BAs via the surface functional groups and substantially mask their detection. If the latter is the case, CDs can also interfere with the absorption of BAs in the gastrointestinal lumen. Further studies are needed to assess this hypothesis.

The degradation and reduction of the content of BAs by novel and effective materials is the strategy of choice in the fermented food. In this work and for the first time, the capability of CDs (as new promising nanomaterial) to degrade four common BAs was investigated. Cadaverine, histamine, tyramine, and putrescine were found to be the most sensitive BAs in the process of reduction/degradation by CDs, respectively. Based on RSM outputs, both CDs concentration and incubation time affected the reduction/degradation of BAs in E. coli and E. faecalis. Two hypotheses were postulated on the mechanism of the reduction/degradation of BAs: 1) the inhibition of the growth and the reduction of BA accumulation, and 2) the degradation of as-prepared BAs into unknown fractions. The results of this study proposed CDs as a potential bio-control material for the reduction of BAs in vitro condition. It is also worthwhile to test CDs on fermented food.

Mahdi Ghorbani: Investigation. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Hossein Tajik: Supervision, Visualization. Fatemeh Salimi: Investigation. Seyedeh Alaleh Kousheh: Investigation. Mahmoud Esmaeili Koutamehr: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, for providing financial support for this work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lwt.2020.110320.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alvarez and Moreno-Arribas, 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The problem of biogenic amines in fermented foods and the use of potential biogenic amine-degrading microorganisms as a solution
Trends in Food Science & Technology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alvarez, M. A., & Moreno-Arribas, M. V. (2014). The problem of biogenic amines in fermented foods and the use of potential biogenic amine-degrading microorganisms as a solution. Trends in Food Science & Technology, 39(2), 146-155. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tifs.2014.07.007

Foods

Journal of Materials Science & Technology
